rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical intramolecular amino- and oxysulfonylation of alkenes with sodium sulfinates affording sulfonylated O- and N-heterocycles (THFs, THPs, oxepanes, pyrrolidines, piperidines, δ-valerolactones)
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of sodium sulfinate (RSO2Na) at the carbon anode generates a sulfonyl radical; subsequent anodic oxidation of the benzylic radical intermediate generates a benzylic carbocation; cathode reduces protons to H2

key_intermediates.name: benzylic carbon-centered radical after sulfonyl addition to alkene
benzylic carbocation after second anodic oxidation

electron_flow: Anode oxidizes sulfinate to sulfonyl radical, which adds to the alkene to give a benzylic radical; the anode further oxidizes this radical to a benzylic cation, which is trapped intramolecularly by the pendant O- or N-nucleophile; protons are reduced at the Pt cathode to H2.

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction of S1 strongly inhibited; P1 formed in only 30% yield and a DPE-sulfonyl adduct (P70) detected by GC-MS, supporting sulfonyl radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of S1, S47, S63 vs p-tolylsulfinate
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peaks of unsaturated alcohol S1 (~1.60 V), N-tosyl amine S47 (~1.65 V), and carboxylic acid S63 (~1.50 V) are higher than p-tolylsulfinate (~1.2 V), indicating sulfinate is oxidized first under electrolysis

notes: DPE was used as a radical trap (analogous role to TEMPO/BHT) rather than a typical persistent radical scavenger. Mechanism is supported by CV ordering of oxidation potentials and trapping evidence; no DFT, KIE, or isotope labeling reported. The benzylic cation pathway (radical–polar crossover) is favored over a direct radical cyclization.
